key_evidence: Microsoft Defender Persistence-category alert with MITRE techniques T1078 and T1098 explicitly identified by the detector.
Registry key, registry value name, and registry value data all present in evidence — consistent with registry-based persistence (T1112).
OAuth application ID included in evidence, raising concern for OAuth app abuse as a persistence vector.
EntityType=User / EvidenceRole=Impacted: a user account is directly involved in or targeted by this activity.
Alert fired at ~03:36 AM local time (Santiago), well outside business hours — anomalous for legitimate user activity.
summary: Microsoft Defender raised a high-confidence Persistence alert on a user entity at 03:36 AM local time, mapping to Valid Accounts (T1078) and Account Manipulation (T1098) with supporting registry modification artifacts and an OAuth application reference. This combination — off-hours timing, account-level manipulation, registry changes, and OAuth app involvement — is consistent with an attacker establishing persistent access after initial compromise of a user account. Tier 2 should immediately resolve the anonymized field IDs (AccountUpn, DeviceName, OAuthApplicationId) in the Defender portal, review the account's recent sign-in history and OAuth grants, inspect the specific registry key and value for known persistence entries (e.g., Run/RunOnce keys), and determine whether the account shows any signs of prior credential theft.
